title: want + someone + to + verb
rule: After 'want', a person is followed by 'to' + base verb.
right: I want you to build it.
examples: I want you to consider re-rendering for optimization.
She wants me to call her tomorrow.
They want us to finish by Friday.

title: talk with / to someone
rule: Use the preposition 'with' or 'to' before a person.
right: I want to talk with people.
examples: Can I talk to you for a second?
I talked with my teacher about it.

title: every + singular noun
rule: 'every' is always followed by a singular noun.

title: make a mistake (not 'do')
rule: We 'make' mistakes, decisions, and plans; we 'do' homework and work.
examples: Don't worry if you make a mistake.
I need to make a decision soon.

title: Articles with singular countable nouns
rule: A singular countable noun needs an article: a / an / the.
wrong: You can use state management library.
right: You can use a state management library.

title: whether (~인지 아닌지)
rule: Use 'whether' (or 'if') to turn a yes/no question into part of a sentence.
wrong: I don't know it will sync.
right: I don't know whether it will sync.
examples: This decides whether your data follows you across devices.
It depends on whether you study every day.

title: preposition + -ing
rule: After a preposition (at, in, about, of, for, on…), use the -ing form, not the base verb.
